So, next I thought, maybe my type has nothing to do with looks. Maybe my real type is men who need me. and more importantly, men who made me feel needed. I thought back on my long history of codependence. As a middle child and de facto mediator in a large family, I found a sense of identity in being the helper, being the facilitator. That's how I felt useful as a kid. It's how I felt lovable. It's how I felt worthwhile at all. Then as an adult, I noticed that I gravitated towards men who needed me to fix something for them, to help them get sober, to motivate them or to be their therapist. title: I Dated outside my "Type". This is what happened.
text: , I noticed that I gravitated towards men who needed me to fix something for them, to help them get sober, to motivate them or to be their therapist. And I steered clear of men that didn't have a clear problem or a deep sadness that I could try to fix. Because if they were happy, then what would they ever want with me? This pattern was largely subconscious for many years of my adulthood. But once I named it, I thought, "Okay, then I'm going to start choosing the very type of men who scare me the most. Men who already have the life they want, men who don't need me." And so began my phase of choosing men who seemed like they had it all going for them. Career, looks, friends, no obvious problem to be fixed. Men who were thriving because their lives were seemingly full and brimming. And they were busy, too. Really busy. I always felt like I was just a few steps away from finally securing their attention with their approval dangled just barely in front of me, like the ever receding end of the rainbow. But if I worked just a little bit harder, made myself just a little sexier, just a little cooler, surely I could finally reach it. So I ran and I ran and I never found where the rainbow touched the earth. At one point he told me he was like, "Yeah, I kind of have this thing where sometimes I'm really really into people and then once they meet me there, I back away." And I was like, "Oh, I mean I mean thank thank you. Thank you for thank you for telling me that you're undatable. That's pretty bad." And that's when I realized I maybe hadn't gotten as far from my type as I thought. These men didn't need me to help them get sober or improve their careers or be their therapist, but they needed me to boost their ego, to make them feel like big, strong men, to make them feel like they were on top of their game and could keep their options open.
